description: The Amur leopard is one of the world's rarest wild cats, found in the temperate forests of the Russian Far East and northeastern China.
endangerment: Decades of habitat loss, illegal poaching for fur, and prey depletion have pushed the wild population to critically low numbers.

description: The Sumatran orangutan is a great ape found only in the northern forests of the Indonesian island of Sumatra.
endangerment: Massive deforestation driven by palm oil expansion and illegal logging has destroyed most of its rainforest habitat.

description: The vaquita is the world's smallest and most endangered marine mammal, endemic to the northern Gulf of California, Mexico.
endangerment: Entanglement in illegal gillnets set for the totoaba fish is the primary driver of its collapse to near extinction.

description: The Javan rhino is the rarest large mammal on Earth, surviving only in Ujung Kulon National Park on the western tip of Java, Indonesia.
endangerment: Historical poaching for horn and severe habitat loss reduced the population to a single tiny refuge. Disease and natural disasters pose ongoing risk.

description: Called the 'Asian unicorn', the saola is an extremely rare bovine discovered only in 1992, living in the Annamite Mountains of Laos and Vietnam.
endangerment: Snare hunting and forest fragmentation from logging roads have made this species one of the most imperilled large mammals in the world.

description: The kākāpō is the world's only flightless parrot, a nocturnal and long-lived bird now surviving only on predator-free islands off New Zealand.
endangerment: Introduced predators — stoats, rats, and cats — devastated populations that evolved without mammalian predators and cannot fly to escape.

description: The Chinese giant salamander is the largest amphibian on Earth, reaching up to 1.8 metres, native to rocky mountain streams in China.
endangerment: Overharvesting for food and traditional medicine, combined with water pollution and dam construction, has caused catastrophic wild population collapse.

description: The hawksbill sea turtle is a vital part of coral reef ecosystems, feeding on sponges that would otherwise overgrow the reef.
endangerment: Long hunted for their beautiful shells used in 'tortoiseshell' products, they also face nesting beach loss, bycatch, and coral reef degradation.

description: The whale shark is the world's largest fish, a gentle filter-feeder that roams tropical oceans and is critical to marine ecosystem health.
endangerment: Targeted fishing and fin trade, bycatch, vessel strikes, and ingestion of marine plastic debris all contribute to significant population decline.

description: Rafflesia produces the world's largest individual flower, up to one metre in diameter, living entirely as a parasite inside rainforest vine roots.
endangerment: Destruction of Sumatran and Bornean lowland rainforest for agriculture eliminates both the plant and its host vine.

description: Mountain gorillas live in the cloud forests of the Virunga volcanoes spanning Rwanda, Uganda, and DRC, and Bwindi Impenetrable Forest.
endangerment: Ongoing armed conflict, habitat encroachment, poaching, and disease transmission from humans have historically suppressed population recovery.

description: Staghorn coral is a fast-growing branching coral that once dominated Caribbean reefs, providing critical habitat for hundreds of species.
endangerment: Ocean warming causes mass bleaching events, while acidification weakens skeletons. Disease and pollution have wiped out most Caribbean coral cover since the 1980s.

description: The Sumatran tiger is the smallest surviving tiger subspecies, restricted to the Indonesian island of Sumatra.
endangerment: Rapid deforestation for palm oil and paper plantations has reduced and fragmented its forest habitat, while poaching for parts fuels illegal trade.

description: The black rhinoceros is a large browsing herbivore once widespread across sub-Saharan Africa, now surviving in scattered protected reserves.
endangerment: Catastrophic poaching for horn — used in traditional medicine and as a status symbol — caused a dramatic population collapse in the 20th century.

description: The Cross River gorilla is the rarest great ape subspecies, confined to a small cluster of forests on the Nigeria–Cameroon border.
endangerment: Centuries of hunting and forest clearance for agriculture have fragmented their range into isolated patches, making genetic exchange and recovery extremely difficult.

description: The Yangtze finless porpoise is a small, intelligent freshwater cetacean unique to China's Yangtze River system, known for its perpetual 'smile'.
endangerment: Heavy shipping traffic, fishing overpressure, pollution, and dam construction have dramatically reduced the population and fragmented their habitat.

description: The pygmy three-toed sloth is the world's smallest sloth, found exclusively on the tiny Isla Escudo de Veraguas off the coast of Panama.
endangerment: Its entire global population lives on a single small island. Illegal hunting, habitat degradation, and the island's isolation make any disturbance potentially catastrophic.

description: The Ili pika is a tiny, teddy-bear-faced mammal living on bare rocky slopes high in the Tianshan mountains of northwest China.
endangerment: Climate change is shrinking its alpine habitat as rising temperatures push vegetation zones upward, leaving less bare-rock territory at altitude.

description: The California condor is North America's largest flying land bird and a cultural icon, once extinct in the wild but slowly recovering through an intensive captive breeding programme.
endangerment: Lead poisoning from bullet fragments in carcasses is the leading ongoing cause of death. Past threats included hunting and DDT-related eggshell thinning.

description: Spix's macaw is a strikingly blue parrot from the Caatinga scrubland of Brazil, considered extinct in the wild since around 2000, with surviving individuals only in captivity.
endangerment: Trapping for the pet trade and destruction of its gallery-forest habitat along dry riverbeds drove it to wild extinction. Reintroduction efforts are now underway.

description: The Philippine eagle is one of the world's largest and most powerful eagles and is the national bird of the Philippines.
endangerment: Deforestation across the Philippine archipelago has reduced its forest habitat dramatically. It is also illegally shot and captured.

description: The Siberian crane is a snow-white bird that makes one of the longest migrations on Earth, from Siberian breeding grounds to wintering sites in China.
endangerment: Almost the entire world population winters at Poyang Lake, China. Drought, dam-related water level changes, and hunting along the flyway pose severe threats.

description: The regent honeyeater is a critically endangered songbird from southeastern Australia, once abundant and now so rare that wild birds are forgetting their own songs.
endangerment: Clearing of box-ironbark woodland for agriculture has removed the large flowering eucalypts this species depends on for nectar and breeding.

description: The Luristan newt is a strikingly coloured black-and-white salamander found only in a handful of cold, clear streams in the Zagros Mountains of Iran.
endangerment: Collection for the illegal pet trade and drought-related stream drying have reduced this already tiny population further.

description: Rabbs' fringe-limbed treefrog was a large, gliding frog from central Panama, known for paternal care; the last known individual died in 2016.
endangerment: The chytrid fungus pandemic wiped out wild populations with devastating speed. No confirmed wild survivors have been reported since.

description: The Titicaca water frog is one of the world's largest aquatic frogs, living entirely underwater in Lake Titicaca on the Peru–Bolivia border.
endangerment: Pollution from untreated sewage and mining runoff entering the lake, overharvesting for food and traditional medicine, and invasive trout have all taken a toll.

description: The ploughshare tortoise is Madagascar's rarest tortoise, prized for its beautiful dome and a prime target for illegal collection for the exotic pet trade.
endangerment: Poaching for the black-market exotic pet trade is the primary driver. Traffickers use GPS to locate tortoises in the wild, outpacing conservation efforts.

description: The Philippine crocodile is a small freshwater crocodile endemic to the Philippines and one of the most endangered crocodilians in the world.
endangerment: Persecution by local communities, unsustainable fishing practices that reduce prey, and wetland conversion have pushed it towards extinction.

description: The gharial is a slender-snouted fish-eating crocodilian unique to the Indian subcontinent, distinguished by the distinctive bulbous growth on the male's snout.
endangerment: Hunting, egg collection, river damming, sand mining, and pollution have reduced a once-widespread species to a tiny fraction of its former range.

description: The European eel is a remarkable fish that lives in freshwater rivers across Europe before making an epic migration to the Sargasso Sea to spawn and die.
endangerment: Its population has declined by over 90% since the 1980s due to barriers blocking river migration, overfishing, parasites, pollution, and climate-driven changes to ocean currents.

description: The Devil's Hole pupfish lives in one of the most extreme habitats on Earth — a single geothermal pool in a limestone cavern in the Nevada desert.
endangerment: With the entire global wild population confined to one tiny rock shelf, any single disturbance — an earthquake, groundwater pumping, or pollution — could cause instant extinction.

description: The Mekong giant catfish is one of the world's largest freshwater fish, reaching up to 3 metres and 300 kg, and is endemic to the Mekong River basin.
endangerment: Overfishing, dam construction blocking migratory routes, and habitat destruction along the Mekong have caused a drastic population decline over the past few decades.

description: The Wollemi pine is a 'living fossil' conifer discovered only in 1994 in a remote sandstone gorge in the Blue Mountains west of Sydney, Australia.
endangerment: The entire wild population consists of fewer than 100 adult trees in a single secret gorge. Wildfire and introduced root disease are existential threats.

description: The dragon's blood tree is a striking umbrella-shaped tree endemic to Socotra Island, Yemen, producing a vivid red resin that gives it its name.
endangerment: Climate change is altering the monsoon patterns that the trees depend on for moisture. Overgrazing by goats and development are also damaging habitat.

description: The coco de mer produces the world's largest seed — a double coconut that can weigh up to 25 kg — and is found naturally only in Seychelles.
endangerment: Its natural range is now just two islands (Praslin and Curieuse), restricted by human land clearance. Illegal harvest of nuts for their high black-market value continues.

description: The freshwater pearl mussel is one of Europe's longest-lived animals, capable of living up to 130 years, and was once found across clean rivers from the Arctic to Spain.
endangerment: Water pollution from agriculture, fine sedimentation of gravel beds, and the loss of juvenile host fish have caused population crashes across most of its range.

description: Schaus' swallowtail is a large, striking butterfly restricted to tropical hardwood hammock forests in the northern Florida Keys.
endangerment: Habitat loss through development, mosquito control spraying, and hurricane damage have all reduced its fragile remaining hammock habitat and population.

description: Partula snails are tiny tree snails from the islands of French Polynesia that became a symbol of the devastation invasive species can cause.
endangerment: The introduction of the rosy wolf snail as a biocontrol agent in the 1970s backfired catastrophically — the predatory snail wiped out most Partula species within decades.

description: The saiga antelope is a prehistoric-looking steppe animal with a distinctive bulbous nose, adapted to filter dust and warm cold air on the vast grasslands of Central Asia.
endangerment: Mass die-offs linked to unusual weather events have killed hundreds of thousands at a time. Poaching for horns used in traditional medicine has also devastated populations.

description: The Irrawaddy dolphin is a round-headed freshwater dolphin found in rivers and coastal waters across Southeast Asia, including the Mekong and Irrawaddy.
endangerment: Drowning in gillnets, blast fishing, habitat degradation from dam construction, and boat traffic have severely reduced the Mekong subpopulation.

description: The Tapanuli orangutan is the most recently described great ape species, discovered in 2017, and lives in a small patch of highland forest in North Sumatra.
endangerment: Its entire range covers only a few hundred square kilometres and is under pressure from a proposed hydroelectric dam, agricultural encroachment, and illegal hunting.

description: The Hainan gibbon is widely regarded as the world's rarest primate, confined to a single nature reserve on the tropical island of Hainan, China.
endangerment: Extensive logging across Hainan in the 20th century eliminated nearly all suitable forest, reducing the population to a tiny number of family groups.

description: The wild Bactrian camel is a distinct species from its domestic cousin, surviving in some of the most remote and harsh deserts of China and Mongolia.
endangerment: Hunting, competition with domestic livestock for water, and habitat loss to mining and human settlement have severely reduced the wild population.

description: The spoon-billed sandpiper is a tiny wading bird with a distinctive spatula-shaped bill, breeding in Russia and wintering on coastal mudflats of South and Southeast Asia.
endangerment: Rapid loss of tidal mudflat staging and wintering sites to reclamation projects along the East Asian flyway, combined with trapping in wintering grounds, has caused severe decline.

description: The forest owlet is a small, stocky owl found only in a few isolated patches of dry deciduous forest in central India, rediscovered in 1997 after more than a century with no confirmed sightings.
endangerment: Deforestation for agriculture and timber, combined with its naturally small and fragmented range, keep the population critically small.

description: The golden mantella is a brilliantly orange-red poison frog endemic to a small area of central-eastern Madagascar, living on the forest floor near seasonal wetlands.
endangerment: Deforestation has reduced its tiny range to a few isolated fragments, and illegal collection for the exotic pet trade adds pressure to an already fragile population.

description: The axolotl is a permanently aquatic salamander famous for its feathery external gills and remarkable regenerative abilities, native to the lake system beneath Mexico City.
endangerment: The ancient Lake Xochimilco has been drastically reduced by urban expansion and water management, and the few remaining canals suffer from pollution and invasive fish that prey on axolotls.

description: The goliath frog is the world's largest frog, reaching up to 32 cm and 3 kg, found only in a limited stretch of fast-flowing rivers in Cameroon and Equatorial Guinea.
endangerment: Hunting for food and the international pet trade, combined with forest clearance and river alteration near its habitat, have caused a significant population decline.

description: The Komodo dragon is the world's largest living lizard, reaching over 3 metres, and is found only on a handful of islands in eastern Indonesia.
endangerment: Climate change is projected to inundate low-lying habitat, and the species occupies a naturally restricted range; prey depletion and occasional poaching add further pressure.

description: The Burmese star tortoise is a beautifully patterned tortoise endemic to the dry zone of central Myanmar, now nearly eliminated from the wild by hunting.
endangerment: Intensive collection for food and the international pet trade has driven the wild population to near extinction; captive populations in Myanmar are central to ongoing recovery.

description: The Ganges shark is a rare true freshwater shark found in the river systems of the Indian subcontinent, not to be confused with the bull shark that also enters fresh water.
endangerment: Severe overfishing, river pollution, dams blocking movement, and habitat degradation from sand mining have made this species extremely rare throughout its range.

description: The smalltooth sawfish is a large ray characterised by a long toothed rostrum, once widespread across the Atlantic tropics and now reduced to a small refuge off Florida.
endangerment: Its saw-like rostrum entangles easily in fishing nets, and extensive coastal development has destroyed the shallow estuarine nursery habitats juveniles depend on.

description: The tequila splitfin is a small freshwater fish endemic to the Ameca River basin in Jalisco, Mexico, declared extinct in the wild before a successful reintroduction effort.
endangerment: Introduced tilapia and carp outcompeted it, while water extraction and agricultural pollution degraded the springs and streams it once inhabited.

description: The Eastern Cape dwarf cycad is a slow-growing cycad endemic to a very small area of the Eastern Cape, South Africa, and is among the most threatened plants on Earth.
endangerment: Illegal collection for private cycad gardens and the horticultural trade has decimated the tiny wild population; slow reproduction makes recovery extremely difficult.

description: Fadang is a cycad endemic to Guam and the northern Mariana Islands that was once one of the most abundant trees on Guam and is culturally significant to the Chamorro people.
endangerment: The introduced cycad aulacaspis scale insect, first detected in the 2000s, has killed the vast majority of fadang plants across Guam; invasive deer also browse seedlings.

description: Perrier's baobab is one of Madagascar's rarest baobab species, restricted to dry deciduous forest in the far north of the island.
endangerment: Slash-and-burn agriculture has destroyed most of the dry forest in its range, leaving a small and declining number of mature trees with little regeneration.

description: ʻŌlulu is a striking Hawaiian succulent plant that grows on the sheer sea cliffs of Kauai and Nihoa, with a bulbous base and a tuft of leaves topped by yellow-white flowers.
endangerment: The extinction of its native pollinator has left it entirely dependent on hand-pollination by botanists to reproduce; cliff erosion and invasive plants further threaten remaining plants.

description: The Lord Howe Island stick insect is a large, jet-black insect thought extinct for decades after rats arrived on Lord Howe Island, rediscovered in 2001 on the remote sea stack of Ball's Pyramid.
endangerment: Black rats introduced in 1918 eliminated the species from Lord Howe Island itself; the entire surviving wild population clings to a small shrub on Ball's Pyramid.

description: The white abalone is a large sea snail that once carpeted rocky reefs off southern California and Baja California, and was the first marine invertebrate listed as endangered under the US Endangered Species Act.
endangerment: Intensive commercial fishing in the 1970s collapsed the population to levels too sparse for natural reproduction; withering syndrome disease has further hampered recovery.

description: The dwarf wedge mussel is a tiny freshwater mussel once found across dozens of Atlantic-slope rivers in the eastern United States, now reduced to a handful of scattered populations.
endangerment: Water quality degradation from agricultural and urban runoff, sedimentation, and the decline of host fish needed for its larval stage have driven dramatic range contraction.

description: The Sumatran rhinoceros is the smallest and most ancient living rhinoceros, covered in reddish-brown hair, surviving in fragmented forest patches of Sumatra and Borneo.
endangerment: Relentless poaching for horn and massive deforestation have reduced the wild population to fewer than 80 individuals scattered across isolated fragments.

description: The eastern lowland gorilla, or Grauer's gorilla, is the largest subspecies of gorilla, found only in the lowland tropical forests of eastern Democratic Republic of Congo.
endangerment: Armed conflict, mining, and bushmeat hunting have decimated populations; surveys estimate a decline of over 70% since the 1990s.

description: The black-footed ferret is North America's only native ferret species and one of the most endangered mammals on the continent, dependent almost entirely on prairie dogs for food and shelter.
endangerment: Deliberate poisoning of prairie dog colonies eliminated most of its prey base. Sylvatic plague, introduced to North America, now kills both ferrets and prairie dogs.

description: The addax is a pale desert antelope with spiralling horns, exquisitely adapted to survive in the Sahara without drinking water, obtaining moisture from the sparse plants it eats.
endangerment: Unregulated hunting using motor vehicles decimated wild populations; oil exploration activity in Niger further disturbed remaining animals.

description: The pygmy hippopotamus is a small, shy, nocturnal relative of the common hippo, found in the swamps and forests of West Africa, spending much more time on land than its larger cousin.
endangerment: Hunting for bushmeat and widespread deforestation across Liberia, Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Côte d'Ivoire have drastically reduced populations.

description: The aye-aye is the world's largest nocturnal primate, using its elongated middle finger to tap on wood and extract insect larvae, filling the ecological niche of woodpeckers on Madagascar.
endangerment: Habitat loss to slash-and-burn agriculture and logging, combined with persecution due to superstitious beliefs that it is an evil omen, threaten remaining populations.

description: The hirola, or Hunter's hartebeest, is the world's rarest antelope, restricted to a narrow strip of arid grassland on the Kenya–Somalia border.
endangerment: Drought, disease, habitat degradation from overgrazing, and predation pressure have reduced the population to a few hundred animals in the wild.

description: The Ganges river dolphin is functionally blind, navigating entirely by echolocation in the turbid waters of the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Meghna river systems.
endangerment: Bycatch in fishing nets, water pollution, damming of rivers that fragments populations, and hunting for oil have all contributed to severe decline.

description: The greater bamboo lemur is the largest bamboo lemur, endemic to Madagascar and uniquely adapted to eat giant bamboo pith containing toxic cyanide, which it tolerates through special biology.
endangerment: Destruction of bamboo and rainforest for slash-and-burn agriculture, logging, and hunting have reduced the species to a few tiny populations.

description: The Bornean orangutan is one of humanity's closest relatives and the largest arboreal mammal, spending most of its life high in the rainforest canopy of Borneo.
endangerment: Industrial-scale deforestation for palm oil, timber, and mining has destroyed more than half of its habitat since 1950, and illegal hunting continues.

description: The southern muriqui is South America's largest primate, a peaceful, social monkey known for its long limbs and spider-like agility through Atlantic Forest canopy in Brazil.
endangerment: Destruction of Brazil's Atlantic Forest — reduced to less than 12% of its original extent — has fragmented muriqui habitat into tiny isolated patches.

description: The Ethiopian wolf is Africa's most endangered carnivore and the world's rarest canid, restricted to the Afroalpine highlands of Ethiopia where it hunts Afroalpine rodents.
endangerment: Rabies and canine distemper spreading from domestic dogs have caused devastating epidemics. Agricultural encroachment is shrinking its high-altitude habitat.

description: The Tonkin snub-nosed monkey is a striking primate with an upturned nose, found only in a few fragmented forest patches in northern Vietnam, with fewer than 250 individuals estimated in the wild.
endangerment: Hunting for meat, traditional medicine, and deforestation for shifting cultivation have reduced this species to critically small numbers.

description: The riverine rabbit is one of Africa's most endangered mammals, restricted to the dense riverine scrub along seasonal rivers in South Africa's Karoo semi-desert.
endangerment: Conversion of riparian scrub to irrigated cropland has destroyed the specialised habitat this rabbit entirely depends on, leaving tiny fragmented populations.

description: The Cuban solenodon is a small, venomous insectivore that is one of the most ancient surviving mammals in the Caribbean, resembling early Mesozoic insectivores from 76 million years ago.
endangerment: Introduced mongoose and cats prey on this ground-dwelling mammal that evolved without mammalian predators. Deforestation further shrinks its forest refuge.

description: The Amami rabbit is a primitive, dark-furred rabbit restricted to two small Japanese islands — Amami-Oshima and Tokunoshima — where it lives in dense subtropical forest and is considered a living fossil.
endangerment: Introduced mongooses, cats, and dogs prey heavily on this ground-dwelling rabbit; deforestation for forestry and development further reduces available habitat.

description: The Indus river dolphin is a nearly blind river dolphin found only in the lower Indus River of Pakistan, navigating by sophisticated echolocation in murky, sediment-rich waters.
endangerment: A series of barrages on the Indus have fragmented the population into isolated sub-populations. Bycatch, water diversion, and pollution add further pressure.

description: The wild water buffalo is the ancestor of all domestic water buffaloes, the largest bovine in Asia, now surviving in tiny populations in India, Nepal, Bhutan, and Thailand.
endangerment: Hybridisation with feral domestic buffaloes, disease transmission from livestock, habitat loss, and hunting have brought wild populations to fewer than 4,000.

description: The La Plata dolphin, or franciscana, is the only river dolphin to live in saltwater, inhabiting shallow coastal and estuarine waters of southeastern South America.
endangerment: Incidental capture in gillnets set for sharks and other fish is the primary cause of death, with tens of thousands estimated killed over recent decades.

description: The blue-eyed black lemur is one of only two non-human primates with blue eyes, endemic to a small area of northwestern Madagascar and exhibiting striking sexual dichromatism.
endangerment: Slash-and-burn agriculture has destroyed most of the humid forest it depends on; illegal capture for the pet trade and bushmeat hunting add further pressure.

description: The Javan slow loris is a small nocturnal primate with a venomous bite — unique among primates — found only on Java, Indonesia, where it feeds on nectar, gum, and insects.
endangerment: Illegal capture for the exotic pet trade, fuelled by viral internet videos, is the primary threat. Deforestation of Java removes the forest habitat it requires.

description: The Arabian leopard is the smallest leopard subspecies, a pale, elusive predator surviving in the rugged mountains and escarpments of the Arabian Peninsula in tiny numbers.
endangerment: Decline of prey animals due to poaching, direct killing by herders to protect livestock, and habitat disturbance have reduced the wild population to under 200 individuals.

description: The blue-throated macaw is a strikingly beautiful parrot restricted to the Beni savanna of northern Bolivia, nesting in natural cavities in isolated Motacú palm groves.
endangerment: Trapping for the pet trade decimated populations in past decades; today habitat loss from cattle ranching and lack of suitable nest trees remain the primary threats.

description: The Bengal florican is a critically endangered bustard of tall-grass floodplain habitats, known for the dramatic aerial display of males during breeding season.
endangerment: Conversion of floodplain grasslands to agriculture, especially rice paddies, across its range in India, Nepal, and Cambodia has eliminated most habitat.

description: The sociable lapwing is a migratory wading bird that breeds on Kazakhstan's dry steppes and winters in the Middle East, South Asia, and Northeast Africa.
endangerment: Rapid conversion of semi-arid steppe to cropland has reduced breeding habitat; hunting pressure on wintering grounds in Sudan and Syria adds further mortality.

description: The hooded grebe is a stunning black-and-white diving bird breeding only on the remote basalt plateau lakes of Patagonia, Argentina, and wintering on coastal channels of Tierra del Fuego.
endangerment: Introduced trout, mink, and kelp gulls prey on eggs and chicks. American mink have spread to breeding lakes, causing catastrophic breeding failures since the 1980s.

description: Edwards's pheasant is a vibrantly coloured bird endemic to the lowland and mid-elevation forests of central Vietnam, possibly extinct in the wild due to intense hunting pressure.
endangerment: Decades of snare hunting for bushmeat during and after the Vietnam War, combined with deforestation, have left no confirmed wild sightings in years.

description: The marvellous spatuletail is considered the world's most spectacular hummingbird, with elongated, wire-like outer tail feathers tipped with violet discs, unique among all birds.
endangerment: Clearing of forest for coffee and sugar cane cultivation has reduced its tiny range in the Utcubamba Valley of Peru; it is also trapped as a supposedly aphrodisiac ingredient.

description: The night parrot is arguably Australia's most mysterious bird — a small, nocturnal, ground-dwelling parrot of the arid interior that went unconfirmed for over 100 years before rediscovery in 2013.
endangerment: Predation by feral cats, grazing pressure that degrades spinifex habitat, altered fire regimes, and possibly drought have contributed to extreme rarity.

description: The black stilt, or kakī, is the world's rarest wading bird, a jet-black shorebird endemic to New Zealand's South Island river systems, with fewer than 200 adults remaining.
endangerment: Introduced predators — cats, ferrets, stoats, and hedgehogs — prey heavily on eggs and chicks. Hybridisation with the related pied stilt also threatens genetic integrity.

description: The Sulu hornbill is an endangered hornbill found only on the Sulu Archipelago in the southern Philippines, where ongoing armed conflict and deforestation have made surveys almost impossible.
endangerment: Widespread deforestation across the Sulu islands and human disturbance linked to ongoing armed conflict have left this species in a precarious, poorly understood state.

description: Gurney's pitta is a brilliantly coloured ground bird once thought extinct, rediscovered in Myanmar in 2003, with additional populations confirmed in southern Thailand.
endangerment: Near-complete deforestation of lowland forest across peninsular Thailand and Myanmar has reduced this species to a tiny number of individuals in scattered forest remnants.

description: The northern bald ibis is a striking bird with iridescent plumage and a bare red face that once ranged across Europe and the Middle East; today only two wild populations survive in Morocco and Syria.
endangerment: Habitat degradation of foraging grounds, hunting along migration routes, and pesticide contamination of invertebrate prey have reduced the wild population to a few hundred birds.

description: The white-headed duck is a small, stiff-tailed diving duck with a distinctive swollen blue bill, found in scattered wetlands across a broad range from Spain to Central Asia.
endangerment: Hybridisation with the introduced North American ruddy duck poses a genetic threat in Spain; habitat loss and hunting have reduced populations across its range.

description: The blue-crowned laughingthrush is a vibrantly coloured songbird once common across southeast China, now reduced to a single wild breeding site in Wuyuan County, Jiangxi province.
endangerment: Its striking plumage made it a prime target for the cage-bird trade; habitat loss through felling of mature broadleaf trees further reduced its ability to recover.

description: The po'ouli was a unique Hawaiian honeycreeper discovered only in 1973 on Maui; the last known individual died in captivity in 2004, making it almost certainly extinct.
endangerment: Introduced rats and mongooses preyed on nests and adults; avian malaria carried by introduced mosquitoes killed birds moving to lower elevations; habitat degradation by pigs and rats was severe.

description: The yellow-crested cockatoo is a white parrot with a distinctive yellow crest, native to Timor-Leste and the Indonesian islands of Sulawesi, Lombok, and surrounding islands.
endangerment: Massive trapping for the international cage-bird trade in the 1980s–90s, combined with deforestation, reduced wild populations by over 80% across much of its range.

description: The waved albatross is the only tropical albatross, breeding almost exclusively on Española Island in the Galapagos, with a wingspan over two metres and a striking courtship dance.
endangerment: Longline fishing bycatch in the Humboldt Current is the primary cause of adult mortality. Introduced rats on some Galapagos islands also threaten nesting success.

description: The Cebu flowerpecker is a tiny, brilliantly coloured bird endemic to Cebu island in the Philippines, once thought extinct for almost a century before a small population was rediscovered in 1992.
endangerment: Virtually all of Cebu's original forest was cleared for agriculture and settlement; remaining birds cling to tiny forest patches that face ongoing pressure.

description: The olm is a ghostly white cave-dwelling salamander adapted to complete darkness in subterranean river systems of the Dinaric Alps, capable of surviving over 100 years without food.
endangerment: Groundwater pollution from agriculture and industry seeps into the cave systems it inhabits; any spill can be catastrophic to this species with no means of escape.

description: The Hula painted frog was declared the first amphibian extinction in Israel in 1996, only to be spectacularly rediscovered in 2011 in the Hula Valley — now considered a living fossil with no close relatives.
endangerment: Drainage of the Hula swamp for agriculture in the 1950s destroyed most of its wetland habitat. The surviving population in the partially restored Hula Nature Reserve remains tiny.

description: The Panamanian golden frog is a brightly coloured, highly toxic toad that is a national symbol of Panama, communicating with other frogs using visual semaphore-like wave gestures.
endangerment: The chytrid fungus Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis swept through Panama in the early 2000s, apparently wiping out all wild populations; the species survives only in captivity.

description: The lemur leaf frog is a delicate gliding tree frog with large eyes and the remarkable ability to change colour, shifting from bright green by night to brown by day.
endangerment: Chytrid fungus has devastated populations across Costa Rica and Panama; deforestation and climate change further stress remaining individuals in cloud forest fragments.

description: The Mallorcan midwife toad is a tiny, pale amphibian known only from fossil record until a living population was discovered in remote gorges of the Serra de Tramuntana in 1977.
endangerment: Introduced grass snakes and viperine snakes prey on adults; chytrid fungus threatens surviving populations; drought from climate change is reducing stream flows.

description: The mountain chicken is one of the world's largest frogs, named for its chicken-like taste when eaten, endemic to Dominica and Montserrat in the Lesser Antilles.
endangerment: The chytrid fungus arrived on Dominica around 2002, killing over 80% of the population within months. Hunting for food and volcanic activity on Montserrat compound the threat.

description: Hochstetter's frog is one of the world's most primitive frogs, a small amphibian found in seeps and wet forest in the North Island of New Zealand, with ancient lineage unchanged for 150 million years.
endangerment: Introduced rats, stoats, and cats prey on frogs and eggs; land clearing has reduced wetland and stream habitat across the North Island.

description: The phantasmal poison frog is a small, vividly striped Ecuadorian frog whose skin toxins led to the development of a powerful pain-relieving compound — epibatidine — by pharmaceutical researchers.
endangerment: Deforestation for agriculture in the Andean foothills of Bolívar province, Ecuador, combined with chytrid disease, has fragmented and reduced populations significantly.

description: The Baw Baw frog is a small, secretive frog found only on the Baw Baw plateau in Victoria, Australia, where it breeds in sphagnum bogs and cool seeps under forest litter.
endangerment: Chytrid fungus has caused catastrophic population collapse; climate change is warming and drying the montane habitat; introduced deer and pigs trample sphagnum bog habitat.

description: The armoured mistfrog is a small rock-dwelling frog from fast-flowing rainforest streams in the Wet Tropics of Queensland, Australia, not seen since 1991 and possibly extinct in the wild.
endangerment: The chytrid fungus swept through Wet Tropics stream frogs in the late 1980s with devastating speed, eliminating numerous species; no confirmed wild sightings have been made since.

description: The Betic midwife toad is a small, terrestrial toad found only in the Betic mountain ranges of southeastern Spain, where males famously carry the egg strings wrapped around their legs.
endangerment: Chytrid fungus, drought associated with climate change, and habitat modification have caused severe declines across the mountain ranges of Andalusia.

description: The yellow-spotted tree frog is a medium-sized frog once widespread in the Southern Tablelands of New South Wales, now believed to survive in only a handful of sites in the Snowy Mountains.
endangerment: Chytrid disease caused catastrophic declines in the late 1980s; drought from climate change, grazing impacts on wetlands, and introduced trout preying on tadpoles also contribute.

description: The Cuban crocodile is the most terrestrial crocodilian in the world, endemic to Cuba's Zapata Swamp and Lanier Swamp, known for hunting in groups and leaping from water to catch prey.
endangerment: Historical overhunting for skin, now hybridisation with the American crocodile — which dilutes the genetic integrity of the tiny pure population — and habitat loss are ongoing threats.

description: The Siamese crocodile is a medium-sized freshwater crocodile once widespread across Southeast Asia, now reduced to tiny fragmented wild populations, mostly in Cambodia.
endangerment: Hunting for skins and meat, collection of eggs and juveniles for crocodile farms, and loss of wetland habitat have left only an estimated few hundred wild individuals.

description: The leatherback sea turtle is the largest reptile alive, a deep-diving, warm-blooded marvel that can reach 900 kg and migrate thousands of kilometres between tropical nesting beaches and cold polar feeding grounds.
endangerment: Bycatch in longlines and trawls, egg harvesting from nesting beaches, and ingestion of plastic bags mistaken for jellyfish have caused dramatic Pacific population declines.

description: Kemp's ridley is the world's smallest and most endangered sea turtle, famous for its mass nesting events called 'arribadas' almost entirely concentrated on a single beach in Mexico.
endangerment: Drowning in shrimp trawl nets caused catastrophic population collapse in the 20th century. Egg poaching, oil spills, and marine debris continue to impact recovering populations.

description: The Chinese alligator is one of only two living alligator species, a small, nocturnal reptile that once ranged across eastern China but is now confined to a tiny area of Anhui province.
endangerment: Conversion of wetlands to rice paddies and aquaculture ponds has destroyed most of its habitat; historically killed as a pest by farmers, now fewer than 200 wild individuals remain.

description: The Round Island boa is a primitive snake found only on the tiny Round Island off Mauritius, remarkable for its unique jaw structure that allows it to swallow lizards sideways.
endangerment: Introduced goats and rabbits devastated the island's vegetation, destroying soil stability and the habitat of the geckos the boa depends on; both were eradicated in the 2000s.

description: The Madagascan big-headed turtle is the sole surviving member of its ancient family, a large freshwater turtle endemic to western Madagascar distinguished by its massive head.
endangerment: Intensive hunting for food and collection for the international pet trade have decimated populations; wetland habitat destruction adds further pressure.

description: The Roti Island snake-necked turtle is one of the world's most endangered turtles, endemic to a small number of lakes on tiny Roti Island near Timor, Indonesia.
endangerment: Massive overcollection for the international pet trade in the 1990s and 2000s removed enormous numbers from the wild; remaining animals face lake habitat degradation.

description: The spiny turtle is a distinctive forest turtle of Southeast Asia, named for the sharp spines along the edge of its shell which help it blend into leaf litter on the forest floor.
endangerment: Massive collection for the wildlife trade — food markets in China and the international pet trade — combined with forest loss has caused severe population declines across its range.

description: The Armenian viper is a stout, heavily-built viper found in mountain steppes of Armenia, eastern Turkey, and western Iran, sheltering under rocks on steep, south-facing slopes.
endangerment: Collection for the pet trade and persecution by local people who fear its venomous bite, combined with overgrazing that degrades its rocky grassland habitat, have reduced populations.

description: The flat-tailed horned lizard is a camouflage master of the Sonoran and Colorado deserts, nearly indistinguishable from the sand it lives on, and famous for squirting blood from its eyes as a defence.
endangerment: Off-road vehicle use that compacts sand and destroys harvester ant colonies — its primary food — combined with urban sprawl in the Imperial Valley, has severely reduced populations.

description: The beluga sturgeon is the world's largest freshwater fish and the source of the most prized caviar, an ancient species that can live over 100 years and grow to 7 metres.
endangerment: Over a century of intensive caviar harvesting has devastated populations. Dams blocking spawning migrations and pollution of the Caspian and Black Sea basins compound the threat.

description: The European sturgeon once ranged across coastal waters from Norway to the Black Sea, but today a single surviving wild population breeds in the Gironde estuary of France.
endangerment: Historical overfishing, bycatch in coastal nets, river damming, and pollution have reduced the wild population to fewer than 750 adults concentrated in the Gironde–Dordogne river system.

description: The Chinese paddlefish was the world's largest freshwater fish, reaching up to 7 metres, with a distinctive long, paddle-shaped snout; scientists declared it likely extinct in 2022.
endangerment: Overfishing for its prized flesh, dam construction on the Yangtze severing migratory routes, and habitat degradation caused rapid population collapse from the 1980s onward.

description: The pallid sturgeon is a large, pale prehistoric fish endemic to the Missouri and lower Mississippi River systems, one of North America's most endangered native fish.
endangerment: River channelisation, dams, and dredging have altered the channel morphology and flow regimes pallid sturgeon require. Wild reproduction is nearly absent in many river sections.

description: The largetooth sawfish is a large ray with a remarkable toothed rostrum, capable of living in both salt and fresh water, once found across tropical coastlines worldwide.
endangerment: Its saw-like rostrum snags in fishing nets, making bycatch mortality extremely high. Coastal habitat loss has devastated estuarine nursery areas across its former vast range.

description: The shortnose sturgeon is a small sturgeon endemic to the Atlantic coast of North America, spending its life in estuaries and moving into rivers to spawn, listed as endangered since 1967.
endangerment: Dams blocking river migration, historical overfishing, water pollution, and dredging of spawning gravel beds have severely limited recovery of this long-lived species.

description: The Chinese bahaba is a large, deep-bodied croaker found in the coastal waters of the South China Sea and East China Sea, with a swim bladder historically more valuable by weight than gold.
endangerment: The extraordinary value of its dried swim bladder in traditional Chinese medicine has made it one of the most intensively targeted fish; its population has collapsed by over 95%.

description: The arapaima is one of the world's largest freshwater fish, reaching 3 metres, an obligate air-breather that must surface regularly, making it highly susceptible to spear-fishers in the Amazon.
endangerment: Overfishing for its highly valued white flesh — easily targeted because it must breathe air — has caused severe population declines across Amazonian floodplains.

description: The giant freshwater stingray is the world's largest freshwater fish by weight, reaching over 600 kg, found in the rivers and estuaries of Southeast Asia and Borneo.
endangerment: Overfishing, bycatch in commercial nets, and dam construction fragmenting river habitats have caused significant declines; its large size and slow reproduction make recovery very difficult.

description: The longfin mako is a large, fast pelagic shark of tropical and warm-temperate oceans, closely related to the shortfin mako and prized by sport fishers for its speed and leaping ability.
endangerment: Targeted and incidentally caught by longline fisheries for fins and meat, its low reproductive rate — producing only a few pups every few years — makes recovery extremely slow.

description: Attenborough's pitcher plant is one of the world's largest carnivorous plants, named after Sir David Attenborough, discovered in 2009 on a remote Philippine mountain and capable of digesting rats.
endangerment: Found on a single ultramafic mountain summit on Palawan, Philippines, with collectors targeting it for the rare plant trade immediately after its description.

description: Grandidier's baobab is the largest of Madagascar's six endemic baobab species and one of the most iconic trees in the world, with a massive cylindrical trunk that can store thousands of litres of water.
endangerment: Clearing of dry deciduous forest for agriculture has devastated the ecosystem around these ancient trees, and studies show almost no seedling recruitment in recent decades.

description: The jellyfish tree is an extraordinary plant unique to Mahé in the Seychelles, the sole member of its family, named for its fruits that resemble jellyfish and known from fewer than 90 wild trees.
endangerment: Restricted to exposed granite hillsides and rocky inselbergs on a single island; very poor natural regeneration, invasive plant competition, and climate variability threaten all remaining adults.

description: The donut cactus is a tiny, perfectly spherical cactus densely covered in white spines, endemic to a small rocky area in Querétaro state, Mexico, and a prime target for succulent collectors.
endangerment: Illegal collection for the specialist succulent trade has severely reduced wild populations on limestone hillsides; its restricted range makes any collection disproportionately damaging.

description: The Haleakalā silversword is one of the world's most striking plants, a monocarpic rosette of silver leaves that blooms once, sometimes after 50 years of growth, then dies atop Maui's volcanic crater.
endangerment: Introduced goats and deer grazing on young plants, and warming, drying conditions reducing mist frequency at the summit, both threaten recovery of this iconic species.

description: The lady's slipper orchid is Europe's most spectacular wild orchid, with a striking yellow slipper-shaped pouch that traps insects to ensure pollination, reduced to a single native plant in Britain.
endangerment: Centuries of collection by Victorian plant hunters and land drainage destroyed most populations; specialist mycorrhizal soil requirements make reintroduction extremely difficult.

description: Café marron is a small tree from Rodrigues Island in the Indian Ocean — the last known individual in the wild was discovered by a schoolboy in 1980; it now survives in cultivation at Kew Gardens.
endangerment: Clearing of Rodrigues' endemic forest for agriculture and introduced livestock grazing eliminated virtually all native vegetation on the island, leaving just one wild tree.

description: Wood's cycad is the world's rarest plant — no female has ever been found, and all known specimens are clones derived from a single male plant discovered in 1895 in KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa.
endangerment: The sole individual was divided and distributed to botanical gardens; without a female counterpart, the species cannot sexually reproduce and is dependent entirely on vegetative propagation.

description: The titan arum produces the world's largest unbranched inflorescence — up to 3 metres tall — and famously mimics the stench of rotting flesh to attract carrion beetles as pollinators.
endangerment: Intensive conversion of Sumatran lowland and hill rainforest to palm oil plantations has destroyed most of its forest habitat; wild plants are also collected for botanical gardens.

description: The star cactus is a spineless, flattened cactus resembling a sand dollar, found in the Tamaulipan thornscrub along the Texas–Mexico border, highly sought by specialist collectors.
endangerment: Illegal collection for the rare cactus trade and conversion of thornscrub to cropland have reduced wild populations dramatically; remaining US sites are tiny and fragmented.

description: The ghost orchid is a ghostly, leafless orchid with no chlorophyll that lives entirely underground except when flowering, obtaining all nutrients through mycorrhizal fungi, famously unpredictable in its appearances.
endangerment: Believed extinct in Britain for 23 years before rediscovery in 2009, it remains one of Europe's rarest plants. Deforestation and changes in woodland management deplete the fungi it depends on.

description: The Franklin tree is a small flowering tree last seen growing wild in 1803 along the Altamaha River, Georgia — all surviving specimens worldwide are descendants of seeds collected by John and William Bartram.
endangerment: The species was extinct in the wild by the early 19th century, possibly due to a soil-borne pathogen combined with its naturally tiny and restricted range.

description: The American burying beetle is North America's largest carrion beetle, once widespread but now reduced to a handful of sites; known for biparental care of larvae on buried animal carcasses.
endangerment: Loss of forest and grassland habitat, decline of large carrion from overhunting of prairie wildlife, pesticides, and light pollution from urbanisation have all contributed to catastrophic decline.

description: The peacock parachute spider is a large, arboreal tarantula from a small area in Andhra Pradesh, India, with striking metallic-blue legs and one of the most spectacular appearances of any spider.
endangerment: Collected intensively for the exotic pet trade, it is found only in a single area of dry deciduous forest; deforestation for timber and agriculture further reduces its habitat.

description: The rosalia longicorn is a strikingly beautiful pale-blue beetle with long antennae banded in black and white, dependent on large, sun-exposed dead or dying beech trees for its larval development.
endangerment: Loss of old-growth and veteran beech trees across European mountain forests due to forestry practices that remove dead wood has eliminated this beetle from much of its former range.

description: The giant clam is the world's largest bivalve, reaching over 1 metre across and weighing up to 300 kg, a slow-growing reef dweller that hosts symbiotic algae and can live over a century.
endangerment: Overharvesting for meat, shells, and the aquarium trade has eliminated it from large parts of its former range; bleaching events linked to climate change also kill the symbiotic algae it depends on.

description: The queen conch is a large, charismatic marine gastropod with a flared pink lip, once abundant across Caribbean seagrass beds and now overfished to near-collapse throughout its range.
endangerment: Decades of commercial overfishing for its prized flesh and shell has drastically reduced populations across the Caribbean. Habitat degradation from coastal development further limits recovery.

description: The white-clawed crayfish is Europe's only native crayfish in most of its range, a slow-growing, sensitive indicator of clean water quality that has disappeared from much of its former habitat.
endangerment: Introduction of the North American signal crayfish brought crayfish plague, a disease that rapidly kills native crayfish; competition and habitat degradation compound the threat.

description: Lange's metalmark butterfly is a small, strikingly patterned butterfly whose entire world population is confined to a single sandy ridge at Antioch Dunes National Wildlife Refuge in California.
endangerment: Industrial sand mining in the 20th century destroyed most of the Antioch Dunes habitat; the sole host plant — buckwheat — must be managed intensively to sustain the butterfly.

description: The noble crayfish is the largest native European crayfish, once widespread across freshwater systems from Scandinavia to Turkey and a culturally important species in Scandinavian cuisine.
endangerment: Crayfish plague, introduced via North American crayfish species, has devastated populations across Europe; water pollution, habitat loss, and climate warming compound the threat.

description: The scarce large blue butterfly has a remarkable life cycle involving deception of red ant colonies, whose larvae it mimics to gain care inside ant nests where it completes development.
endangerment: Drainage and intensification of wet meadows has eliminated both the great burnet plants its larvae feed on and the specific ant species it parasitises, causing severe range contraction.

description: Triton's trumpet is a large, beautifully patterned marine snail and a crucial predator of crown-of-thorns starfish, which devastate coral reefs when their populations explode.
endangerment: Overcollection for its prized shell — used as a musical instrument, decoration, and souvenir — has severely reduced populations across the Indo-Pacific, indirectly damaging reef health.

description: The American horseshoe crab is a living fossil unchanged for 450 million years, critical to Delaware Bay shorebirds that depend on its eggs during spring migration and to biomedical science for its blood.
endangerment: Overharvesting for bait and biomedical bleeding, combined with coastal development destroying spawning beaches, has reduced populations in some areas by over 90%.

description: The giant Gippsland earthworm can reach 3 metres in length and is audible underground — a gurgling sound as it moves through the soil — endemic to a small area of blue clay soil in Victoria, Australia.
endangerment: Land clearing for dairy farming has converted most of its specialised moist blue clay hillside habitat; soil compaction and drainage from livestock add further pressure.

description: The thick-shelled river mussel is a freshwater bivalve that was once common across clean rivers of central Europe, now declining severely due to water quality degradation and loss of host fish.
endangerment: Agricultural nutrient pollution causing eutrophication, fine sediment from erosion smothering gravel beds, and decline of the salmonid host fish its larvae require have caused catastrophic range contraction.

description: The pink mucket is a freshwater mussel once widespread across major rivers of the eastern United States, now reduced to a handful of rivers where water quality remains adequate for its survival.
endangerment: Industrial and agricultural water pollution, channelisation and impoundment of rivers, and decline of the host fish needed for larval development have caused catastrophic population loss.

description: The capricorn diving beetle is the largest diving beetle in Europe, a powerful predatory aquatic insect of clean, large lakes with abundant aquatic vegetation, now vanished from most of its historical range.
endangerment: Eutrophication and acidification of Scandinavian and central European lakes, combined with drainage of bog pools, has eliminated the clear, vegetation-rich water habitats it depends on.

description: The southern damselfly is a small, brilliant blue damselfly restricted to chalk and limestone streams with flowing, clean, base-rich water and abundant aquatic vegetation in southern England and western Europe.
endangerment: Agricultural intensification causing nutrient runoff, stream modification, and loss of traditional water meadow management have eliminated it from most former sites in the UK.

description: The pied tamarin is a small primate endemic to the urban surroundings of Manaus, Brazil. It is remarkable for persisting in a fragmented landscape of city parks, gardens and forest remnants surrounded by a sprawling city of two million people.
endangerment: Urban expansion of Manaus has eliminated most forest habitat, while road networks fragment remaining patches and vehicle collisions kill tamarins attempting to cross between fragments.

description: The black-and-white ruffed lemur is one of Madagascar's largest lemurs and a key seed disperser of large-seeded rainforest trees. Its loud, raucous calls carry far through the canopy.
endangerment: Slash-and-burn agriculture, illegal logging, and cyclone disturbance have devastated eastern Madagascar's rainforests, causing a 95% reduction in ruffed lemur populations over three generations.

description: The pygmy three-toed sloth lives exclusively on Isla Escudo de Veraguas, a tiny island off Panama's Caribbean coast. It is the world's smallest sloth, 40% smaller than its mainland relatives, and found nowhere else on Earth.
endangerment: The entire world population, estimated at fewer than 100 individuals, is confined to a single 4.3 km² island, making it exceptionally vulnerable to any disturbance including mangrove degradation and human visitation.

description: The sei whale is the third largest baleen whale, capable of reaching 20 metres and speeds of 50 km/h. Once one of the most abundant great whales, it was devastated by 20th-century industrial whaling.
endangerment: Over 200,000 sei whales were killed during the whaling era of the 1960s–70s alone. Recovery is hampered by entanglement in fishing gear, ship strikes, and climate-driven changes to prey availability.

description: Stresemann's bristlefront is a secretive, ground-dwelling bird of the Atlantic Forest in Bahia, Brazil. Known from fewer than ten confirmed sightings over 60 years, it may be the world's most elusive bird.
endangerment: The Atlantic Forest of Bahia has been reduced to less than 11% of its original extent, and the remaining patches continue to be degraded by charcoal production and cattle ranching.

description: The Raso lark is the world's rarest lark, endemic to the tiny uninhabited islet of Raso in the Cape Verde archipelago. The total population fluctuates between about 40 and 1,500 birds depending on rainfall.
endangerment: Extreme vulnerability to drought on a 7 km² island with no fresh water means the entire species can be devastated by a single bad dry season; climate change is intensifying drought frequency and severity.

description: Archey's frog is one of the most primitive living frogs, unchanged for 70 million years. It is tiny — less than 4 cm — lacks a tympanum, and carries eggs on its back rather than spawning in water. Found only on New Zealand's Coromandel Peninsula.
endangerment: The global population has crashed by 88% since the 1990s due to chytridiomycosis — a fungal skin disease devastating amphibians worldwide — combined with predation by introduced rats and stoats.

description: Lehmann's poison frog is one of the most beautiful and toxic frogs in the world, with bold red and black markings warning predators of its potent skin alkaloids. Endemic to a tiny area of Pacific slope forest in Cauca, Colombia.
endangerment: Its extremely small range in the Anchicayá and Naya river valleys is under intense pressure from deforestation for coca cultivation and timber, while collection for the international pet trade has depleted wild populations.

description: The Table Mountain ghost frog exists nowhere else on Earth but the ravines of Table Mountain above Cape Town. Its entire global range covers just a few square kilometres on the southwestern slopes of this iconic mountain.
endangerment: Invasive pine plantations consume water from the mountain's streams, while winter tourist pressure disturbs breeding sites; climate change is reducing rainfall and altering the cold, fast-flowing streams the species requires.

description: The Jamaican iguana is Jamaica's largest native land animal, a large, grey-green lizard once found island-wide. Thought extinct for 40 years, it was rediscovered in the Hellshire Hills in 1990, where a few hundred still survive.
endangerment: Invasive mongooses, rats, cats, and pigs eat iguana eggs and hatchlings, allowing fewer than 2% of eggs to reach adulthood; remaining dry limestone forest habitat is cleared for charcoal production.

description: The geometric tortoise is the rarest tortoise in southern Africa, named for the striking geometric star pattern on its shell. It is confined to the Swartland lowlands of the Western Cape, South Africa, in a landscape almost entirely converted to agriculture.
endangerment: More than 96% of its lowland fynbos habitat has been converted to wheat fields and vineyards; the remaining fragments are too small and isolated to sustain viable populations, and urban expansion continues.

description: The Alabama cavefish is the rarest cave fish in North America, known only from a single cave pool in Key Cave, Alabama. This completely blind, depigmented fish has never been seen in any other location.
endangerment: The species depends entirely on bat guano from cave-roosting grey bats as its base food source; declines in bat populations from white-nose syndrome are reducing the food supply, while agricultural pesticide runoff pollutes the cave pool.

description: The Mangarahara cichlid is a freshwater fish from a small river system in northeastern Madagascar. The Mangarahara River is believed to have dried up completely, and the species was declared extinct in the wild around 2013.
endangerment: The Mangarahara River in Mahajanga province has been dammed for irrigation and extraction, causing the river to dry up seasonally; the last wild individuals disappeared when the river failed entirely.

description: The Clanwilliam cedar is a majestic conifer that once covered large swathes of the Cederberg mountains in the Western Cape. Now reduced to scattered, mostly elderly individuals, the species is failing to regenerate in a warming, fire-prone landscape.
endangerment: More frequent and intense wildfires, driven by climate change and invasive pine that fuels hotter fires, kill adult trees before seeds can mature; surviving trees are mainly old individuals with poor regeneration rates.

description: The St. Helena ebony is a small flowering tree endemic to the remote island of St. Helena in the South Atlantic. Once extinct in the wild, it was rescued from total extinction by cuttings grown in cultivation and subsequently replanted on the island.
endangerment: Introduced goats eliminated all native St. Helena forest over centuries; by 1998, the species was considered extinct in the wild with only a handful of cultivated individuals remaining.

description: Wallace's giant bee is the world's largest bee, with a wingspan of 6 cm and large mandibles used to gather tree resin. Known only from a few Indonesian islands in North Maluku, it was lost to science for 38 years until its rediscovery in 2019.
endangerment: Deforestation for cocoa, oil palm, and logging has destroyed most of the lowland forest habitat on Bacan, Obi, and Halmahera islands; the species' nest sites in occupied termite mounds are destroyed with the trees they inhabit.

description: The monarch butterfly undertakes one of nature's most extraordinary migrations, travelling up to 4,800 km from Canada and the USA to overwintering colonies in the oyamel fir forests of central Mexico. The migratory population was added to the IUCN Red List as Critically Endangered in 2022.
endangerment: Loss of milkweed through herbicide use and agriculture in North America has removed the only plant monarch caterpillars can eat; climate extremes disrupt migration; illegal logging at overwintering sites removes refuge trees.

description: The Indiana cave crayfish is a pale, eyeless freshwater crustacean adapted to the perpetual darkness of limestone cave streams in south-central Indiana. It represents an ancient lineage isolated underground since the Pleistocene.
endangerment: Agricultural runoff carrying pesticides, sediment, and fertilisers seeps into the karst groundwater system, poisoning cave streams; groundwater extraction for crop irrigation lowers water tables, drying cave pools.

description: The baiji, or Yangtze River dolphin, was a freshwater dolphin endemic to the Yangtze River in China. A 2006 survey failed to detect any individuals, and it is now considered functionally extinct — the first cetacean driven to extinction in modern times.
endangerment: Intensive river traffic, electrofishing, dam construction blocking migration, and industrial pollution destroyed the Yangtze ecosystem on which the baiji depended. A 1997 estimate counted only 13 individuals; by 2006, none could be found.

description: The North Atlantic right whale is among the world's most endangered large whales, with fewer than 370 individuals remaining. Once targeted by whalers for its high blubber content and tendency to float when killed, giving it its name as the "right" whale to hunt.
endangerment: Rope entanglement in fishing gear — particularly lobster trap lines — and vessel strikes kill several whales every year, a rate that exceeds reproduction. Climate change is shifting prey into areas with higher fishing activity.

description: The roloway monkey is a striking primate with a distinctive white beard and dark face, found in the Upper Guinea forests of Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Surveys suggest it may be functionally extinct in Ghana with only a few remaining populations in Côte d'Ivoire.
endangerment: Bushmeat hunting, with roloway monkeys considered a delicacy in local markets, has devastated populations across both countries; simultaneous deforestation for cocoa farming has eliminated remaining habitat corridors.

description: The pygmy hog is the world's smallest wild pig, barely 25 cm tall and weighing around 8 kg. Once distributed across the Terai grasslands from Nepal through Bhutan to Assam, it now survives only in Manas National Park and a handful of adjacent reserves in Assam, India.
endangerment: The conversion of tall elephant grass and terai swamp habitat to agriculture and human settlement eliminated the species from all but a fraction of its range; annual burning destroys remaining grassland patches.

description: The Brazilian merganser is a large, serrated-billed diving duck and one of South America's rarest birds. Estimated at fewer than 250 mature individuals, it occupies fast-flowing, clear rivers in the Brazilian Cerrado.
endangerment: Siltation from agriculture, hydroelectric dam construction, and destruction of riparian gallery forest have devastated the clear, fast-flowing rivers this specialist species depends on for feeding and nesting.

description: The blue-eyed ground dove is an exquisite small dove with iridescent blue eyes, endemic to the Brazilian Cerrado. Believed extinct for 75 years, it was spectacularly rediscovered in 2015 in a tiny fragment of native Cerrado in Minas Gerais.
endangerment: The Brazilian Cerrado has lost more than half its natural vegetation to agriculture; native campo limpo grassland — the specific habitat this dove needs — is among the most threatened vegetation types in South America.

description: The Marquesan kingfisher is found only on the island of Tahuata in the Marquesas Islands, French Polynesia. With an estimated population of just 400–600 individuals, it represents one of the Pacific's most threatened forest birds.
endangerment: Introduced cats and rats predate eggs and chicks from nesting cavities; habitat loss from agriculture and invasive vegetation transforms the native coastal and valley forest the species depends on.

description: The Negros bleeding-heart is a ground-dwelling pigeon named for the vivid red "blood spot" on its white breast. Restricted to the islands of Negros and Panay in the Philippines, it is among the most threatened birds in Southeast Asia.
endangerment: Deforestation for agriculture has eliminated more than 95% of lowland forest on Negros and Panay; remaining forest is heavily hunted, and the species is also captured for the cagebird trade.

description: The Mississippi gopher frog is a stocky, warty frog that uses the burrows of gopher tortoises for shelter in longleaf pine forests of the US Gulf Coast. Fewer than 200 adults survive at a single natural breeding pond in Harrison County, Mississippi.
endangerment: The near-total loss of longleaf pine savanna and open-canopy forest — less than 3% of the original extent remains — combined with fire suppression that closes canopy over breeding ponds, has driven this species to the edge of extinction.

description: The purple frog is a blobby, dark-purple amphibian with a tiny pointed head, endemic to the Western Ghats of India. A relic lineage that separated from its closest relatives over 100 million years ago, it lives almost entirely underground, emerging only briefly to breed.
endangerment: Conversion of the Western Ghats' evergreen forests to monoculture plantations — particularly cardamom and coffee — destroys the deep, moist soil habitat where the frog spends 11 months of the year; chytridiomycosis is an emerging threat.

description: Jackson's climbing salamander is a brilliantly coloured yellow-and-black amphibian that was known only from museum specimens for 42 years until its rediscovery on cloud forest trees in the Cuchumatanes Mountains of Guatemala in 2017.
endangerment: The cloud forest of the Sierra de los Cuchumatanes in Huehuetenango is under intense pressure from illegal logging and slash-and-burn agriculture; chytridiomycosis threatens remaining populations.

description: The Yangtze giant softshell turtle is the largest freshwater turtle on Earth, with a shell over a metre long. With fewer than four confirmed living individuals — including one female artificially inseminated in 2019 — it is among the most critically endangered animals on the planet.
endangerment: Hunting for food and traditional medicine, sand dredging, pollution, and dam construction across the Yangtze and Red River systems destroyed this turtle's habitat and population over the 20th century; the last confirmed wild female died in 2019.

description: The spider tortoise is a small, attractively patterned tortoise endemic to the spiny dry forests of southwestern Madagascar. It can retract into its hinged shell and aestivate for months during drought, but its numbers have collapsed in recent decades.
endangerment: Poaching for the illegal international pet trade combined with habitat destruction from charcoal production and slash-and-burn agriculture in Madagascar's spiny forest has caused severe population declines.

description: The Jamaican boa is Jamaica's largest terrestrial predator, a yellow-and-black constrictor reaching two metres. It controls rodent populations and pollinates flowers by serving as a nocturnal pollinator for certain plants. Persecution, road kills, and habitat loss have made it rare.
endangerment: Deliberate killing by humans who fear or dislike snakes, combined with predation of eggs and young by introduced mongooses and rats, and forest clearing for agriculture have severely reduced populations across Jamaica.

description: The four-eyed turtle gets its name from the two large, eye-like ocelli on the back of its head that may confuse predators. Native to mountainous streams of southern China and northern Vietnam, it is among the region's most traded turtles.
endangerment: Intensive collection for Chinese food and traditional medicine markets has devastated populations; habitat degradation from dam construction, sand mining, and stream channelisation has further reduced available habitat.

description: The spotted handfish is a remarkable fish that "walks" across the seafloor on modified pectoral fins rather than swimming. Endemic to the lower Derwent Estuary in Tasmania, Australia, it is one of the world's most endangered marine fish.
endangerment: Introduction of the Pacific seastar has devastated populations by consuming the stalked ascidians the handfish uses as nest supports; siltation, pollution, and habitat degradation in the Derwent Estuary compound the threat.

description: The Siamese tiger perch is a large, boldly striped freshwater fish from the Chao Phraya and Mekong river systems in Thailand. Prized by aquarium collectors and as a food fish, it has virtually vanished from its native rivers.
endangerment: Overfishing for food and the aquarium trade, dam construction blocking migration routes, and severe water pollution in Thai river systems have reduced populations to near extinction in the wild.

description: The Brazilian guitarfish is a large elasmobranch with a body shaped between a shark and a ray, once abundant in the warm shallow coastal waters of southern Brazil and Uruguay. Industrial fishing has reduced it by more than 80% in recent decades.
endangerment: High-value bycatch in shrimp trawls and longline fisheries, combined with targeted fishing for its large fins, has devastated populations; the shallow estuaries and nursery areas it needs for pups are heavily impacted by coastal development.

description: The monkey puzzle tree is a living fossil conifer from the Andes and coastal mountains of Chile and Argentina, with interlocking triangular leaves that persist for up to 24 years. Sacred to the Pehuenche people, its large seeds — piñones — are a critical food source.
endangerment: Past commercial logging devastated old-growth stands; now the Patagonian fire cycle, amplified by climate change, and invasive exotic species threaten remaining populations; cattle grazing prevents regeneration in many areas.

description: The toromiro is a small flowering tree that was the last surviving native tree of Easter Island (Rapa Nui) when the last wild individual died around 1960. Seeds saved by Thor Heyerdahl in 1956 allowed propagation in botanical gardens worldwide; reintroduction is now underway.
endangerment: Polynesian settlers cleared Easter Island's original forest for agriculture and introduced rats ate seeds; introduced sheep and cattle then prevented regeneration; the final wild tree died in the 1960s.

description: Palo de rosa is a tall Amazonian tree whose heartwood produces a highly prized essential oil — rosewood oil — used in perfumery and once a key ingredient in Chanel No. 5. Decades of destructive extraction by cutting and distilling entire trees have driven it to near extinction in the wild.
endangerment: Rosewood oil distilleries require around 60 felled trees per tonne of oil; despite a Brazilian export ban, illegal extraction continues in remote Amazonian forests near Manaus and along the Tapajós River.

description: The Oʻahu tree snail is one of 41 native Achatinella species, the celebrated arboreal tree snails of the island of Oʻahu, Hawaii. Their beautifully coloured, banded shells were historically collected into extinction; today introduced predators have eliminated most remaining populations.
endangerment: The introduced wolf snail Euglandina rosea — itself released as a biocontrol agent — hunts Achatinella species to extinction one population at a time; rats, chameleons, and fire kill remaining snails; habitat loss from urbanisation compounds the threat.
